config.title: Spreewald Day Trip: FAQ
config.subtitle: Straight answers on getting to the Spreewald from Berlin by train, what punts and kayaks cost in 2026, whether you need to book, and how much fits into one day.

items.q: How do I get to the Spreewald from Berlin without a car?
items.a: Take a direct regional train to Lübbenau. RE2 runs hourly and stops at Berlin Ostkreuz and Gesundbrunnen; RE7 runs on the same corridor from Ostbahnhof and Ostkreuz. Between them there are usually two direct trains an hour, and the ride from Ostkreuz takes about an hour. Neither line serves Hauptbahnhof or Alexanderplatz, so start from Ostkreuz.

items.q: How much does a Spreewald punt tour cost in 2026?
items.a: At the Großer Kahnfährhafen in Lübbenau, the published 2026 prices are 17 euros for the two hour Lehde tour (children 4 to 11 pay 9), 20 euros for the three hour classic with a stop in Lehde, and 28 euros for the 5.5 hour Wotschofska forest tour. Longer special tours and evening punts cost more and need a reservation.

items.q: Should I take a punt tour or rent a kayak?
items.a: A punt seat and a two hour kayak rental cost about the same, so it is a question of style. The punt is zero effort: you sit, the ferryman poles and talks, the route is fixed. A kayak is quiet and free to stop anywhere, and the water is flat enough for complete beginners. My advice: first visit with limited time, take the three hour punt. Anyone who likes being on the water even a little, rent the kayak.

items.q: Do I need to book a Spreewald punt in advance?
items.a: Not for the standard Lehde tours. The boats have no timetable: they board from about 10:00 to 11:30 and each punt leaves when it fills. Reservations matter for the 10:00 Wotschofska tour, the themed and evening punts, and for groups. Kayaks are first come, first served; on summer weekends arrive when the rental stations open around 9:00.

items.q: Is one day enough for the Spreewald?
items.a: For Lübbenau and Lehde, yes. A train around 9:00 from Ostkreuz puts you on the water by 11:00, leaves time for the village, the open-air museum and a pickle stop at the harbor stalls, and gets you back in Berlin by early evening. What one day does not cover is the deeper network around Burg or the quieter Unterspreewald; that is overnight territory.

items.q: Does the Deutschlandticket work for the Spreewald?
items.a: Yes. RE2 and RE7 are regional trains, so the 63 euro monthly Deutschlandticket covers the whole ride. Without one, note that Lübbenau lies outside the Berlin ABC zones, so a normal Berlin ticket is not enough. From two people upward the Brandenburg-Berlin-Ticket for 36.50 euros a day, valid for up to five, is usually cheaper than singles; check the exact single fare in the DB Navigator app.

items.q: When is the best time for a Spreewald day trip?
items.a: The punt and kayak season runs roughly April to October, and the Lehde open-air museum is open daily from late March to the end of September from 10:00 to 18:00, then to 17:00 in October. In high summer the difference between a calm day and a crowded one is simple: weekday instead of weekend, and on the water before noon.
